Welcome

Welcome to Minestarters

Minestarters is a tokenized mining investment protocol delivering exploration-stage exposure through curated, diversified vaults — ring-fenced within a Bermuda Segregated Accounts Company, accessible via on-chain vault tokens, with secondary market liquidity from day one.

Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026

Abstract →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
Abstract

1. Abstract

Exploration is where mining value is created and where the capital model is most broken. A single failed drill result can erase 60-80% of a junior\'s market value overnight. Private placements routinely trade at 10-30% illiquidity discounts. Geological due diligence demands specialist knowledge that most investors simply do not have in-house. And capital, once deployed, can sit locked for years with no credible exit pathway. These are not edge cases. They are the structural reality of a sector where 75% of companies deliver negative shareholder returns over extended periods, yet the energy transition demands hundreds of billions in new mineral supply within the decade.

Minestarters was built to fix this. We are a tokenized mining investment protocol that packages exploration-stage exposure into curated, diversified vaults each holding multiple geologist-selected projects, ring-fenced within a Bermuda Segregated Accounts Company, and accessible through on-chain vault tokens issued by a DABA-licensed platform. The structure is designed around a single thesis: that institutional-quality exploration exposure can be delivered with built-in diversification, embedded geological expertise, transparent on-chain valuation, and critically secondary market liquidity from day one.

The vault model preserves the discovery torque that makes exploration attractive. A basket of curated projects does not dilute upside into noise if one project delivers a 10-20x outcome, its impact on vault NAV is meaningful. What the vault eliminates is the binary concentration risk of backing a single junior, the compliance friction of holding frontier-market equity directly, and the illiquidity trap that has suppressed institutional capital allocation to exploration for decades.

Secondary liquidity is delivered through Asset-Referenced Tokens and a protocol-managed AMM pool, enabling real price discovery and tradeable positions before any underlying project reaches production or exit. This is a structural capability that does not exist in direct junior equity today.

This whitepaper sets out the protocol architecture, custody framework, due diligence methodology, tokenomics, and regulatory structure behind Minestarters and the case for why exploration vaults represent a new asset class for qualified investors.

← WelcomeOverview →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE PROBLEM / The Problem: Overview

2. The Problem: Junior Mining's Broken Capital Model

The world needs more mines. Copper demand is projected to exceed supply by 5-8 million metric tons by 2030. Nickel faces a shortfall of up to one million metric tons. For those two metals alone, meeting demand could require \$250-350 billion in cumulative capital expenditure this decade. Yet the industry responsible for making these discoveries is financed through a model that actively discourages the capital it needs.

Institutional investors face four structural barriers that suppress allocation to junior exploration: a financing model that penalises both miners and their backers (2.1), a set of investor-side challenges that make the asset class operationally hostile to hold (2.2), and a near-total absence of secondary liquidity that locks capital for years with no credible exit (2.3). These are not inefficiencies waiting to be optimised. They are foundational failures in how exploration capital is raised, deployed, and recovered and they explain why the sector remains starved of investment at the precise moment global demand requires it to scale.

← AbstractFinancing Gap →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE PROBLEM / Financing Gap

2.1 The Junior Mining Financing Gap

The financing options available to a junior explorer today are, without exception, extractive. Every path to capital comes with structural costs that erode the very value the capital is meant to create. We see four compounding barriers that keep the mining financing model broken not at the margins, but at its foundation.

Valuation discount for illiquidity. When an institutional investor writes a cheque into a private junior mining placement, they price in an illiquidity discount because they cannot easily exit their position. The explorer bears the cost of that discount as a lower entry valuation even if the underlying asset is exceptional. This is not a negotiating tactic; it is a structural feature of a market with no credible secondary exit. The explorer starts every funding round already underwater on value.

Single backer, single point of control. A large cheque from one institutional investor buys more than equity. It buys disproportionate leverage: over valuation, dilution terms, royalty structures, governance rights, board seats, and veto powers. The project\'s trajectory can become hostage to a single set of interests. For an explorer whose geological thesis may take years to prove, this concentration of control can be as dangerous as concentration of risk.

A tiny addressable investor pool. Direct institutional mining placements reach a small, well-connected, and geographically concentrated universe of accredited investors primarily in North America, Australia, and London. Global retail capital, family office capital outside these corridors, and the broader qualified investor base are simply unreachable through traditional placement channels. The result is a funding market that is shallow by design, not by necessity.

The compliance burden falls on the explorer. KYC/AML screening, investor verification, cross-jurisdictional securities compliance, and ongoing reporting obligations all fall on the mining company\'s team. For an exploration-stage company, this administrative overhead consumes management time and budget that should be going into the ground. Every dollar spent on compliance infrastructure is a dollar not spent on drilling.

These barriers compound into a financing environment where dilution, not geological failure, is the single largest destroyer of shareholder value in early-stage mining. Exploration companies are funded by investors, not revenues, and require constant follow-on investment at uncertain terms. The traditional model forces repeated capital raises, each one dilutive, each one subject to whatever terms the market will bear at that moment in the commodity cycle.

The macro picture reinforces the dysfunction. Mining capital efficiency lags the technology sector by a factor of three. Mining productivity remains roughly 25% below mid-2000s levels. Global mining capex fell from \$260 billion to \$130 billion between 2012 and 2020, hollowing out project pipelines at the precise moment the energy transition demands they be full. The traditional financing options equity dilution through private placements at steep discounts, streaming and royalty agreements that surrender long-term upside, debt facilities with onerous covenants are not solutions to this problem. They are symptoms of it.

The average junior miner spends 7-12 years and \$10M-50M+ navigating from initial exploration through a bankable feasibility study, with no guarantee of success. The capital model that is supposed to fund this journey instead penalises the companies undertaking it.

← OverviewInvestor Challenges →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE PROBLEM / Investor Challenges

2.2 Investor-Side Challenges

The financing model described above fails miners. It fails investors equally but for different reasons. Even a qualified investor with appetite for exploration exposure faces a set of structural barriers that make the asset class operationally hostile to hold.

Concentration risk with no built-in diversification. A single junior exploration stock carries binary outcome risk geological results are pass/fail, and a failed programme can destroy the majority of a company\'s market capitalisation in a single session. Building a properly diversified exploration portfolio in-house requires significant time, specialist knowledge, and assets under management that most allocators are unwilling to dedicate to a single sub-sector. There are over 2,000 early-stage mineral resource companies listed globally, yet every conventional route to exposure is a standalone, single-project bet.

The discovery expertise gap. Geological due diligence demands specialist knowledge that most institutional mandates do not have in-house. Evaluating drill results, resource estimates, metallurgical studies, and jurisdictional permitting risk requires a skillset that sits outside traditional financial analysis. Project selection is the single most important variable in exploration investing backing the wrong geological thesis destroys capital regardless of commodity prices or market conditions.

Deep illiquidity with no exit pathway. Private placements and micro-cap exploration positions routinely trade at illiquidity discounts of roughly 10% for Western Australian listings, 25-30% for West African exposure, and exceeding 30% for locked private placements in frontier markets. Capital can be locked for 3-5+ years with no credible exit, particularly where a project has not yet reached a development-stage catalyst. For an investor who needs to rebalance, mark to market, or report NAV to their own LPs, this is not an inconvenience, it is a disqualifying structural feature.

Opacity and operational friction. Traditional mining investment structures offer limited visibility into project progress between quarterly reports. Investors commit capital and then operate in an information vacuum geological data arrives irregularly, NAV movements are opaque, and there is no standardised framework for tracking milestones across a portfolio. This is compounded by the custody and regulatory complexity of holding frontier-market equity directly cross-jurisdictional securities treatment, beneficial ownership reporting, and tax classification add operational cost that suppresses allocation before a single geological question is asked.

High entry thresholds. Even where an investor clears the expertise, compliance, and illiquidity hurdles, minimum investment sizes for meaningful exploration exposure typically start at \$250K-\$5M+. This excludes the vast majority of qualified investors globally and creates a self-reinforcing cycle: a narrow capital base leads to worse terms for miners, which further discourages new entrants.

The cumulative effect is not underallocation at the margins. It is systematic exclusion of an entire investor class from an asset category that, when accessed well, can deliver 5-10x re-ratings as projects progress along the development curve.

← Financing GapThe Liquidity Desert →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE PROBLEM / The Liquidity Desert

2.3 The Liquidity Desert

The illiquidity problem runs deeper than discounts and lock-ups. It is a structural absence. There is no secondary market for exploration-stage mining equity, not a thin one, not an inefficient one, but effectively none at all.

This is worth understanding precisely, because it is the single feature that most distinguishes exploration from other alternative asset classes. Private credit has structured redemption windows. Real estate has REIT wrappers and secondary LP markets. Even venture capital, an asset class defined by illiquidity has developed a growing secondary market with dedicated intermediaries and standardised transfer mechanics. Exploration equity has none of these. An investor who commits capital to a junior mining placement cannot exit until one of three things happens: a trade sale, a public listing, or a write-off. There is no in-between.

The consequences cascade through the entire capital model.

Price discovery fails. Without a secondary market, there is no continuous mechanism for establishing what a position is worth between funding rounds. An investor\'s only reference points are the last placement price which may be stale by months or years and whatever geological newsflow the company has released. Marks are subjective, comparables are sparse, and the gap between intrinsic value and realisable value can be enormous. For investors who must report NAV to their own stakeholders, this is not merely inconvenient; it makes the asset class difficult to hold within any standard portfolio reporting framework.

Capital allocation becomes distorted. When there is no exit, the decision to invest becomes binary: full commitment or zero exposure. There is no ability to take a small position and scale in as conviction builds, or to trim a position that has run ahead of fundamentals. Every allocation is a one-way commitment with no adjustment mechanism. This pushes conservative allocators who might otherwise deploy meaningful capital to the sidelines entirely.

The illiquidity discount compounds over time. Entry discounts do not remain static. If an investor needs to exit before a liquidity event, they face an even steeper haircut or no bid at all. The longer the hold period, the deeper the effective discount, because the probability of needing to exit for portfolio-level reasons increases with time. This creates a perverse dynamic: the most patient capital, which is best suited to exploration timelines, is the capital most penalised by the absence of liquidity.

The sector-level effect is capital starvation: Every barrier already discussed valuation discounts, dilutive fundraising, narrow investor pools, compliance friction, concentration risk is amplified by the absence of secondary liquidity. If investors could trade exploration positions, they would accept lower entry discounts. If miners could point to a liquid secondary market, they could raise capital on better terms. If portfolio managers could rebalance, they could allocate more. The liquidity desert is not one problem among many. It is the multiplier that makes every other problem worse.

There is over \$3 trillion worth of listed junior mining and exploration companies globally. The geological opportunity exists. The investor appetite, conditional on the right structure, exists. What does not exist is a mechanism for converting committed exploration capital into a tradeable, price-discoverable position before a project reaches exit.

That is the structural gap and it is the specific problem the Minestarters vault architecture was designed to solve.

← Investor ChallengesMining Market Size →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
MARKET OPPORTUNITY / Mining Market Size

3.1 Junior Mining Market Size

Global mining exploration expenditure was approximately \$12.7 billion in 2024, with junior miners accounting for 30-40% of total spend. This is the sector\'s R&D budget, the capital that finds tomorrow\'s mines. And it is structurally insufficient for what the next decade demands.

The disconnect between exploration spend and required supply is stark. The energy transition alone requires massive new volumes of copper, nickel, lithium, cobalt, and rare earth minerals that do not yet have sufficient discovered reserves to meet projected demand. Exploration is the only way to close that gap. Yet global mining capex remains roughly 30% below its 2013 peak, project pipelines are thin across the critical metals, and the financing dysfunction continues to suppress the capital available to the companies doing the actual discovery.

The investable universe is large. There are over 2,000 early-stage mineral resource companies listed globally across TSX-V, ASX, LSE-AIM, and other exchanges. The Minestarters pipeline alone built through three active curator networks already includes 15 projects across gold, copper, lithium, helium, and manganese, spanning Mauritania, Zambia, Ghana, the United States, Mexico, Morocco, and Botswana, with a combined pipeline capital value exceeding \$3 billion.

But size alone does not create opportunity. What creates opportunity is the combination of a large, underserved asset class with a structural change in how capital can be raised and deployed. The convergence of two forces, the critical mineral supercycle driving demand for new discoveries, and the maturation of tokenised real-world asset infrastructure enabling new capital formation models creates a window that did not exist five years ago.

← The Liquidity DesertRWA Tokenization Growth →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
MARKET OPPORTUNITY / RWA Tokenization Growth

3.2 RWA Tokenization Growth

The infrastructure required to tokenise real-world assets at institutional scale now exists. It did not five years ago.

The tokenised RWA market crossed \$30 billion in Q3 2025, led by private credit (\~\$17B) and U.S. Treasuries (\~\$7.3B). Tokenised money market funds have scaled past \$2 billion in AUM within eighteen months of launch. Structured credit pools have crossed \$1 billion in TVL. These are not experiments. They are functioning capital markets infrastructure regulated, custodied, audited, and holding real institutional money. The total RWA tokenisation market is projected to reach \$10-16 trillion by 2030.

What matters for Minestarters is not the headline figure. It is what the growth pattern reveals about which asset classes are next.

Tokenization has followed a clear progression: it started with the safest, most liquid, most standardized assets, government securities and short-term credit because those were the easiest to custody, price, and regulate on-chain. Each successive wave has moved one step further along the risk and complexity spectrum, into private credit, structured products, and real estate. The pattern is consistent: first prove the rails work for simple assets, then extend them to asset classes where the transparency, fractionalisation, and liquidity benefits of tokenisation create the most value.

Mining exploration sits at the far end of that spectrum: high complexity, long duration, specialist valuation, frontier-market custody challenges which is precisely why it has not been tokenised yet, and precisely why the opportunity is largest. The tokenised commodity segment today is approximately \$2 billion, dominated entirely by physical gold and silver tokens that provide commodity price exposure with no connection to exploration upside. No existing tokenisation infrastructure has been purpose-built for exploration-stage mining investment. No protocol offers diversified vault exposure, integrated secondary liquidity, or mining-specific NAV tracking.

The rails now exist. The regulatory precedents have been set. The institutional comfort with on-chain asset custody has been established. What remains is applying this infrastructure to the asset class that needs it most and where the structural value creation from tokenisation (liquidity, transparency, diversification, global access) is greatest.

← Mining Market SizeCompetitive White Space →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
MARKET OPPORTUNITY / Competitive White Space

3.3 The Competitive White Space

To understand where Minestarters sits, it is worth surveying what already exists because the gap becomes obvious only when you see what the current landscape actually offers.

Tokenised mining and commodity protocols: A small number of projects have attempted to bring mining or commodity assets on-chain. Enegra (EGX) has tokenised 100% of the equity in a Malaysian commodity trading company with offtake agreements across 16 mid-tier miners in Southeast Asia, issuing an ERC-3643 compliant security token on Polygon. It is the largest mining-adjacent tokenisation by reported NAV but it offers exposure to a single company\'s trading business, not to diversified exploration upside. Ayni Gold has linked a token to the processing throughput of a single gold mine in Peru. CONSA, working through the Brickken tokenisation platform, has issued a token backed by a single Chilean salt mining operation. Silver Scott Mines has announced RWA tokenisation intentions but has not launched a live product.

BlackFe (\$BLKFE) has tokenised Philippine mineral royalties, issuing 100 billion tokens on Polygon backed by reported agreements worth \$4.6 billion. The model converts royalty streams from Philippine mining operations into digital assets, a royalty-pass-through structure focused on a single jurisdiction\'s regulatory framework. It is production-stage revenue tokenisation in one country, not exploration-stage equity investment across a diversified portfolio.

In every case, the pattern is the same: one asset, one company, one commodity or one jurisdiction, no portfolio diversification, and no integrated secondary liquidity.

Tokenised commodity tokens: Paxos Gold (PAXG), Tether Gold (XAUT), and Kinesis dominate the tokenised precious metals market, providing commodity price exposure backed by physical metal held in vaults. But these tokens have no connection to mining exploration. Holding PAXG is economically equivalent to holding a gold bar; it captures none of the discovery upside, geological optionality, or development-stage re-rating that characterises exploration investment.

RWA infrastructure protocols: BlackRock\'s BUIDL fund operates through Securitize as transfer agent with BNY Mellon as custodian. Ondo Finance delivers tokenised Treasury yields through OUSG and USDY. Centrifuge has built pool-based infrastructure for tokenised private credit. Goldfinch pioneered a decentralised credit underwriting model for emerging-market lending. Each has validated a piece of the infrastructure stack regulated custody, on-chain NAV, pool-based diversification, decentralised curation but none operates in the mining exploration vertical. Their asset classes are fundamentally different: short-duration, yield-bearing, and relatively standardised. Exploration is long-duration, binary-outcome, and requires specialist geological assessment that no general-purpose RWA platform is equipped to provide.

Where the gap is. The market landscape divides cleanly along two axes: asset specificity and portfolio structure.

Every mining-specific protocol Enegra, Ayni, CONSA, BlackFe, Silver Scott is a single-asset or single-jurisdiction token. Every diversified, institutional-grade RWA protocol BUIDL, Ondo, Centrifuge, Goldfinch operates outside mining. No existing protocol combines mining-domain expertise with diversified vault-based portfolio construction, integrated secondary market liquidity, and institutional custody infrastructure.

That is the quadrant Minestarters occupies and it is currently uncontested. The opportunity is not to build a slightly better version of what exists. It is to build the category.

← RWA Tokenization GrowthConvergence Thesis →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
MARKET OPPORTUNITY / Convergence Thesis

3.4 Convergence Thesis

Two structural forces are converging for the first time and their intersection defines the Minestarters opportunity.

The first is the critical mineral supercycle. The energy transition requires copper, nickel, lithium, cobalt, and rare earths in quantities that existing discovered reserves cannot supply. Demand is not speculative; it is embedded in the capital expenditure plans of automakers, battery manufacturers, grid operators, and governments worldwide. Exploration is the only way to close the gap between projected demand and known supply. Yet the companies doing the actual discovery remain locked out of the capital they need by a financing model that penalises them at every stage.

The second is the institutional maturation of RWA tokenisation. Tokenised assets have scaled past \$30 billion, with regulated custody, on-chain NAV, compliant issuance, and institutional capital now standard features of the infrastructure. The technology has been proven. The regulatory precedents have been set. The question is no longer whether real-world assets can be brought on-chain; it is which asset classes benefit most from the transition.

Mining exploration is the answer to that question. It is the asset class where illiquidity is deepest, where transparency is weakest, where access barriers are highest, and where the structural benefits of tokenisation, fractionalisation, secondary liquidity, on-chain auditability, and global distribution create the most value. No other asset class combines the same severity of capital formation failure with the same magnitude of structural improvement available through tokenisation.

The window is open now. Five years ago, the RWA infrastructure did not exist. Five years from now, the first-mover position in tokenised mining exploration will be occupied. Minestarters is building to occupy it.

← Competitive White SpaceOverview →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE SOLUTION / The Solution: Overview

4. The Minestarters Solution

Minestarters is a tokenised mining investment protocol purpose-built for exploration-stage assets. It is designed around a conviction that has shaped every architectural decision: that exploration upside the most asymmetric return profile in natural resources can be delivered to qualified investors without requiring them to accept the concentration risk, illiquidity, opacity, and compliance friction that have historically defined the sector.

The platform does not retrofit generic financial infrastructure onto mining. It builds from the geology up embedding specialist curation into the investment process, structuring custody around the specific legal requirements of cross-jurisdictional mining equity, and engineering secondary market liquidity for an asset class that has never had it.

The following subsections describe each component of the architecture: how vaults are constructed and diversified (4.1), how cash flows reach investors (4.2), how the investment lifecycle operates from formation through exit (4.3), how liquidity is created before any underlying project reaches production (4.4), how vault valuations are tracked and reported (4.5), and how these components combine in an illustrative return scenario (4.6).

← Convergence ThesisVault-Based Diversification →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE SOLUTION / Vault-Based Diversification

4.1 Vault-Based Diversification

Each Minestarters vault is a curated basket of exploration-stage mining projects typically five or more selected by specialist geologists and structured to provide exposure across multiple commodities, jurisdictions, and exploration stages within a single tokenised vehicle.

How a vault is constructed. The Minestarters incubator team evaluates projects through a rigorous due diligence funnel and assembles each vault around a diversified thesis. A vault might hold a West African gold explorer alongside a Latin American copper-gold project, a lithium play in an established mining jurisdiction, and two earlier-stage targets in frontier geographies. Capital is allocated across the portfolio based on each project\'s specific requirements and negotiated equity terms not equally weighted, but sized according to risk, stage, and capital need. Each vault raises a target amount determined by the portfolio\'s aggregate capital requirements, with the majority deployed into mining equity and a defined portion allocated to seed the vault\'s liquidity infrastructure.

Discovery torque is preserved. The vault holds enough projects to spread risk but few enough that a single strong outcome meaningfully moves the portfolio. If one project delivers a major discovery the kind of event that can re-rate an explorer by an order of magnitude that return is not diluted into statistical noise by a hundred other positions. This is the critical design trade-off: broad enough to survive multiple failures, concentrated enough that success matters. Even assuming a high capital loss probability across the portfolio, a single high-multiple discovery can drive the vault to a competitive institutional-level return.

Diversification across multiple dimensions. Vault construction deliberately diversifies across commodity type (gold, copper, lithium, base metals), geography (reducing single-jurisdiction political and regulatory risk), and project stage (blending earlier-stage exploration targets with more advanced projects approaching resource definition). This multi-axis diversification means that no single drill result, no single government decision, and no single commodity price movement can define the vault\'s outcome.

What the vault eliminates. The binary concentration risk of backing a single junior explorer. The dependency on a single commodity cycle. The exposure to a single jurisdiction\'s permitting and political regime. The requirement for investors to independently source, evaluate, and monitor individual exploration positions across a fragmented global market of over 2,000 listed companies. The vault packages all of this into a single, professionally managed allocation with the curation, custody, compliance, and liquidity infrastructure described in the sections that follow.

← OverviewRevenue Participation Agreements →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE SOLUTION / Revenue Participation Agreements

4.2 Revenue Participation Agreements & Cash Flow Structure

A vault is only as valuable as the mechanism that routes returns back to its investors. In traditional mining finance, this is where structures break down, cash flows are irregular, distributions depend on board discretion, and reporting timelines stretch to quarters or years. Minestarters replaces this with a purpose-built instrument: the Revenue Participation Agreement.

What an RPA is. A Revenue Participation Agreement is a contractual arrangement between the vault\'s custody entity and each underlying mining project, negotiated at the point of capital deployment. It defines the economic terms under which the vault participates in the project\'s value creation specifying what cash flows the vault is entitled to, under what conditions, and through what settlement mechanism.

What RPAs capture. Each RPA is structured to capture the full spectrum of value an exploration project can generate as it progresses along the development curve: royalty income linked to production, revenue shares from pilot production or offtake agreements, and exit proceeds from trade sales, joint venture buy-ins, or public listings. The RPA ensures that the vault\'s economic participation is defined upfront, documented on-chain, and enforceable not subject to renegotiation at each funding round or dependent on a project management team\'s discretion.

How cash flows reach token holders. When a cash flow event occurs, proceeds are routed through the vault\'s custody structure and settled to vault token holders proportionally and automatically via smart contract. Project-level documentation that triggers or validates payments is verified through trusted oracle services before settlement executes. Every distribution is permanently auditable on-chain. There is no manual reconciliation and no intermediary deciding when or whether to distribute.

← Vault-Based DiversificationFive-Phase Lifecycle →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE SOLUTION / Five-Phase Lifecycle

4.3 Five-Phase Investment Lifecycle

Every vault follows a consistent lifecycle from project selection through to investor distribution. The five phases are designed to move capital from commitment to deployment to return in a structured, transparent sequence with each phase building on the governance and custody infrastructure described in later sections.

— Figure 1: Phase 1 --- Incubation & Project Selection —

Phase 1: Incubation & Project Selection. The Minestarters incubator identifies and evaluates exploration-stage companies seeking capital. Projects are assessed on geological merit, management capability, jurisdictional risk, and capital efficiency through a multi-stage due diligence funnel. The output is a curated portfolio of typically five or more projects for a single vault. The current pipeline spanning gold projects in Mauritania and Zambia, copper targets in Mexico and Morocco, lithium in Ghana, helium in the United States, and manganese in Botswana illustrates the commodity and geographic range that vault construction targets. Not every pipeline project enters a vault; the conversion rate from application to vault inclusion is designed to be well below one in ten.

— Figure 2: Phase 2 --- Vault Opening & Fundraising —

Phase 2: Vault Opening & Fundraising. Once the portfolio is finalised, the vault opens for subscription. Qualified investors deposit capital and receive vault tokens proportional to their contribution. The fundraise runs until the vault\'s target is met, a target determined by the aggregate capital requirements of the selected projects. There is no open-ended accumulation; each vault raises what its portfolio needs and closes.

— Figure 3: Phase 3 --- Capital Deployment to Custody —

Phase 3: Capital Deployment. Committed capital is deployed into the portfolio companies through the vault\'s custody structure. Equity positions are acquired and RPAs are executed with each project, establishing the contractual terms under which cash flows will return to the vault. The deployment phase transforms investor capital into real-world mining equity held within a ring-fenced legal structure at which point the vault\'s NAV begins tracking the progress of its underlying projects.

— Figure 4: Phase 4 --- Secondary Market Liquidity (AMM) —

Phase 4: Active Management & Secondary Liquidity. Once deployed, the vault enters its operational phase. The incubator team monitors project progress drill results, resource estimates, permitting milestones, management execution and the NAV tracking engine updates vault valuations accordingly. Simultaneously, the vault\'s liquidity infrastructure activates: vault token holders gain access to secondary market trading through Asset-Referenced Tokens and the protocol-managed AMM. This phase can last several years, reflecting the natural timeline of exploration through development.

— Figure 5: Phase 5 --- Exit Event & Profit Distribution —

Phase 5: Exit & Distribution. When a portfolio company achieves a liquidity event a trade sale, a joint venture transaction, a public listing, or a royalty buyout the proceeds flow back through the custody structure and are distributed to vault token holders proportionally via smart contract. Distributions are settled in USDC and recorded permanently on-chain. A vault may generate multiple distribution events over its life as different projects reach different outcomes at different times. When all projects have reached resolution, the vault closes.

← Revenue Participation AgreementsLiquidity Architecture →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE SOLUTION / Liquidity Architecture

4.4 Liquidity Architecture: ARTs, AMM & Secondary Markets

The absence of secondary liquidity is the single greatest structural barrier to institutional capital in exploration. Minestarters addresses this through a multi-layered liquidity architecture designed to deliver real price discovery and tradeable positions from the point of vault deployment not years later when a project reaches exit.

The architecture operates through four complementary mechanisms.

Asset-Referenced Tokens (ARTs). ARTs are synthetic tokens that reference the pooled mining asset value of a vault without representing direct equity. They enable early liquidity by allowing trading based on mining asset valuations rather than requiring project-level exits. Mining assets reprice at technical inflection points drill results, resource updates, feasibility milestones and ARTs capture these revaluations in a tradeable instrument. This provides vault participants with a liquid position that reflects the evolving value of the underlying portfolio, even while the projects themselves remain pre-production.

Permissioned AMM. The Minestarters AMM is a decentralised exchange providing continuous secondary market liquidity for vault tokens. Only KYC-verified participants can trade, maintaining regulatory compliance while enabling real price discovery. The AMM is seeded at vault launch with an initial liquidity allocation, ensuring that trading depth exists from day one rather than developing organically over months.

Multi-source liquidity depth. The AMM draws liquidity from three distinct sources to maintain market depth across varying conditions. Vault-Owned Liquidity (VOL) is seeded from a defined portion of each vault\'s fundraise at deployment. Protocol-Owned Liquidity (POL) is maintained from the \$MINE treasury, providing a baseline floor of market depth that persists even in low-activity periods. Liquidity Partner Liquidity (LPL) is contributed by external liquidity providers who earn incentives for deepening the pool.

\$MINE Liquidity Assurance. The \$MINE protocol token provides an additional liquidity layer including protocol-owned treasury depth, liquidity provider incentives, and backstop mechanisms ensuring that vault token holders always have access to a credible exit even in low-activity periods.

The combined effect is a structural liquidity premium over equivalent locked exploration equity. Junior mining positions routinely trade at significant illiquidity discounts. If the secondary market is credible real trading depth and genuine price discovery that discount inverts into a premium for vault token holders. This is not a theoretical benefit. It is a measurable structural uplift that accrues directly to investors.

Note: Technical specification for AMM pricing curves, liquidity incentive parameters, ART minting/redemption mechanics, and POL bond programme design is in preparation to be published as a companion document prior to platform launch.

← Five-Phase LifecycleNAV Tracking & Valuation →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
THE SOLUTION / NAV Tracking & Valuation

4.5 NAV Tracking & Valuation

Exploration assets are notoriously difficult to value there is often nothing tangible to price beyond geological potential, management quality, and the probability that further work will prove or disprove a thesis. Most mining investment structures handle this by not handling it at all: investors receive quarterly PDF reports with stale marks and subjective commentary. Minestarters takes a fundamentally different approach.

— Figure 8: NAV Tracking Process —

The valuation engine. The NAV Tracking Module monitors and calculates the Net Asset Value of vault holdings by analysing 48+ document types from mining companies across the major reporting jurisdictions NI 43-101 and SEDAR in Canada, S-K 1300 and SEC EDGAR in the United States, and JORC/PERC standards in Australia and Europe. The engine applies multiple valuation models depending on each project\'s stage and data availability: market-based comparable transactions, in-situ resource valuations derived from published resource estimates and prevailing commodity prices, Lassonde Curve stage-mapping that tracks where each project sits on the characteristic exploration-to-production valuation curve, and discounted cash flow analysis using stage-appropriate discount rates.

Stage-appropriate risk pricing. We do not use the 5-8% discount rates that are standard in NI 43-101 economic studies. Those rates systematically understate the risk of exploration-stage ventures and inflate project NPVs in ways that mislead capital allocation. Our NAV engine applies time-varying hurdle rates that start high in early exploration reflecting the genuine probability of failure at that stage and decline as projects de-risk through technical milestones, resource definition, and feasibility work. This is how venture capital has priced early-stage risk for decades. There is no reason mining exploration should be valued differently.

Update frequency. NAV updates occur at multiple frequencies: real-time for commodity price movements affecting in-situ valuations, monthly for full portfolio recalculation incorporating new geological data and project milestones, and quarterly for audited comprehensive reports. Vault token holders see their position\'s value evolve continuously not in 90-day snapshots.

Value versus price. The NAV engine is designed to estimate intrinsic value based on geological data, stage progression, and defensible financial models not to track the speculative price that the market may assign to an exploration story at any given moment. In exploration, these two numbers can diverge dramatically. Our job is to give investors the data-driven value estimate and let them make their own judgement about whether the market price represents an opportunity or a risk.

4.6 Illustrative Example: Hypothetical Vault Returns

The following scenario illustrates how the vault model performs under different outcome distributions. All figures are illustrative; only actual vault sizes, project allocations, and returns will vary based on each vault\'s specific portfolio and market conditions. This is not a projection.

Base assumptions. A hypothetical vault raises \$12M and deploys capital across five exploration-stage projects. An assumed 65% capital loss probability is applied across the portfolio consistent with the historical failure rate in early-stage exploration. Returns are shown gross of platform fees. The assumed realisation period is five years.

Bear case -- 0.8x MOIC. Four projects fail completely and are written off. One project delivers a moderate 5x return. Gross proceeds: approximately \$10M on \$12M deployed. The vault does not return invested capital. This scenario highlights a critical reality of exploration investing: without at least one high-multiple discovery event, diversification alone cannot overcome the base rate of project failure. Even a 5x winner is insufficient if the rest of the portfolio produces nothing.

Base case -- 3.3x MOIC. Three projects fail completely. One project delivers a moderate 5x return. One project delivers a major discovery outcome at 15x. Gross proceeds: approximately \$40M on \$12M deployed. This scenario delivers a competitive institutional-level IRR consistent with private exploration benchmarks - driven not by the average performance of the portfolio, but by the convexity of a single high-multiple discovery alongside one moderate success. This is the return profile the vault is designed to capture.

Bull case -- 5x MOIC. Three projects fail completely. One project delivers a 10x return. One project delivers an exceptional 20x discovery outcome. Gross proceeds: approximately \$60M on \$12M deployed. Two strong outcomes generate venture-style returns while diversification limits the damage from three complete write-offs. This scenario demonstrates the asymmetric upside that makes exploration compelling as an asset class and why preserving discovery torque within the vault structure is a non-negotiable design principle.

What the scenarios reveal. In all three cases, the majority of projects fail. That is not a flaw in the model, it is the reality of exploration, and any honest underwriting must start from that premise. The vault\'s value proposition is not that it eliminates failure. It is that the portfolio structure allows a single outsized success to carry the entire vault to a meaningful return, while spreading the cost of failure across multiple positions rather than concentrating it in one.

The difference between the bear and base case is a single project moving from 0x to 15x. The difference between base and bull is the magnitude of the top performer. In exploration investing, the distribution of outcomes is not normal it is heavily skewed. The vault is built to capture that skew.

← Liquidity ArchitectureSmart Contract Architecture →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
PROTOCOL ARCHITECTURE / Smart Contract Architecture

5.1 Smart Contract Architecture

The smart contract layer governs the complete vault lifecycle from investor deposit through capital deployment, ongoing management, and exit distribution.

Vault contracts manage token issuance upon investor deposit, capital routing to the custody structure, and pro-rata distribution of returns when cash flow events occur. Capital deployment to portfolio companies operates through milestone-gated logic: funding is released in tranches as projects achieve predetermined technical and commercial milestones. If a project fails to reach an agreed milestone, subsequent funding stages are withheld automatically. This mechanism replaces manual oversight and protracted renegotiation with programmable, transparent capital discipline.

The AMM contract manages the permissioned liquidity pool, processing trades between vault tokens and USDC within defined parameters. Access control ensures that only KYC-verified addresses can interact with the pool. The NAV oracle contract ingests verified valuation data and updates per-token NAV on-chain, providing continuous price reference for both AMM operations and investor reporting.

Contracts are deployed on an EVM-compatible chain using upgradeable proxy patterns with timelocks 48 hours for contract-level changes ensuring that no unilateral modification can occur without transparent advance notice. All contracts will undergo independent third-party security audits prior to mainnet deployment.

Note: Full smart contract specification including function-level documentation, access control matrices, and audit reports will be published as a companion technical document prior to platform launch.

← NAV Tracking & ValuationVault Token Design →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
PROTOCOL ARCHITECTURE / Vault Token Design

5.2 Vault Token Design

— Figure 6: Vault Token Lifecycle —

Vault tokens are ERC-1400 compliant security tokens representing economic rights to the profits generated by a vault\'s underlying mining equity portfolio. They explicitly do not confer equity ownership, governance rights, or voting power in any corporate entity.

Each vault issues its own token series. Total supply equals the vault\'s raised amount at a 1:1 par value with USDC a vault raising \$12M issues 12 million tokens. Tokens are minted upon investor deposit during the funding phase and burned upon refund (if a vault fails to reach its funding target) or upon vault termination and final distribution.

Vault tokens are transferable between qualified investors and tradeable on the permissioned AMM after vault deployment. Per-token NAV is updated continuously via the NAV oracle, providing holders with a real-time reference value for their position. The ERC-1400 standard supports partition-based transfer restrictions, enabling compliance controls including whitelist enforcement, jurisdictional blocking, and holding period requirements where applicable.

[Note: Full token specification including transfer restriction logic, partition design, and redemption mechanics will be published as a companion technical document prior to platform launch.]{.mark}

← Smart Contract ArchitectureAMM & Secondary Liquidity →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
PROTOCOL ARCHITECTURE / AMM & Secondary Liquidity

5.4 AMM & Secondary Market Liquidity

The Minestarters AMM is a decentralised exchange providing continuous secondary market liquidity for vault tokens, the first such mechanism built specifically for tokenised mining exploration assets.

The AMM is seeded with a defined allocation of USDC and vault tokens from each vault\'s fundraise at deployment. This ensures that trading depth exists from day one. The pool operates on a permissioned basis only addresses that have completed KYC verification can execute trades maintaining regulatory compliance without sacrificing the price discovery and accessibility benefits of decentralised exchange infrastructure.

Liquidity depth is maintained through three layers:

VOL (Vault-Owned Liquidity): Seeded from each vault\'s initial fundraise allocation. This is the foundational liquidity layer it exists from the moment the vault deploys and persists for the vault\'s lifetime.

POL (Protocol-Owned Liquidity): Maintained from the \$MINE treasury. POL provides a baseline floor of market depth across all vaults, ensuring that even in periods of low trading activity, vault token holders have access to a credible exit mechanism.

LPL (Liquidity Partner Liquidity): Contributed by external liquidity providers who earn \$MINE incentives for deepening AMM pools. LPL supplements VOL and POL, creating additional depth that scales with protocol adoption.

Additionally, Asset-Referenced Tokens (ARTs) provide synthetic liquidity referenced to pooled commodity asset value, enabling pre-discovery cash flows and early-stage trading activity. The \$MINE Liquidity Assurance Layer provides protocol-level backstop mechanisms and incentive programmes to maintain minimum market depth across all active vaults.

[Note: AMM pricing curve design, fee parameters, liquidity incentive mechanics, ART minting/redemption specifications, and POL bond programme structure are in preparation to be published as a companion technical document prior to platform launch.]{.mark}

← Vault Token DesignVault Tokens →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
TOKENOMICS / Vault Tokens

6.1 Vault Tokens (Per-Vault)

Each vault issues its own ERC-1400 vault token series. Total supply equals the vault\'s total raised amount at 1 token = 1 USDC par value at issuance. Vault tokens represent pro-rata economic rights to all cash flows generated by the vault\'s portfolio royalties, revenue shares, milestone payments, and exit proceeds routed through Revenue Participation Agreements and settled on-chain. Vault tokens are the primary instrument through which investors hold exposure to the curated exploration portfolio and are tradeable on the permissioned AMM.

← AMM & Secondary Liquidity$MINE Protocol Token →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
TOKENOMICS / $MINE Protocol Token

6.2 \$MINE Protocol Token

\$MINE is the native protocol token of the Minestarters ecosystem. It serves four primary functions:

Access: \$MINE holders gain access to early-bird vault subscription opportunities, where vault pricing may incorporate dynamic mechanisms rewarding early commitment.

Liquidity incentivisation: \$MINE rewards are distributed to AMM liquidity providers through the POL bond programme, incentivising sustained market depth across vault pools. Token holders can stake \$MINE for defined periods 3, 6, 9, or 12 months earning yield paid in \$MINE tokens.

Protocol-level backstop: \$MINE staking contributes to the liquidity assurance layer, providing a structural floor for AMM depth.

Governance: \$MINE holders participate in protocol governance decisions covering fee parameters, curator approval, and vault-level policy.

Demand for \$MINE is reinforced through platform usage, and supply management mechanisms including buyback programmes, token burns, and utility sinks are designed to align circulating supply with protocol growth and value creation.

[Note: The full \$MINE token economics paper including total supply, allocation schedule, vesting terms, emission curve, staking yield parameters, and governance mechanics is in preparation and will be published as a standalone appendix prior to the token generation event.]{.mark}

← Vault TokensFee Structure →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
TOKENOMICS / Fee Structure

6.3 Fee Structure

The fee model is structured around a core principle: Minestarters earns when vaults earn.

Platform fees are charged on successful capital deployment and vault-level returns, not on assets under management. This alignment eliminates the incentive to accumulate capital for its own sake and keeps the protocol focused on net returns to vault participants. General and administrative overhead is designed to remain well below 15% of deployed capital, the threshold above which institutional investors typically flag capital drag as a disqualifying concern.

The fee structure spans three entities: the Minestarters Incubator (Dubai) charges a fee on vault size for project sourcing and due diligence services, the Platform (Bermuda) collects operational fees for token issuance and AMM operation, and the Custody Top Co (Bermuda SAC) charges custody and fiat gateway fees for asset administration.

[Note: The specific fee schedule including percentage rates per entity, performance fee mechanics, and curator compensation structure will be finalised and published ahead of Vault 1 launch.]{.mark}

← $MINE Protocol Token$MINE Liquidity Assurance →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
TOKENOMICS / $MINE Liquidity Assurance

6.4 \$MINE Liquidity Assurance Layer

The \$MINE protocol token provides baseline liquidity assurance for all active vault pools through four mechanisms: a Protocol-Owned Liquidity treasury ensuring minimum market depth, \$MINE liquidity rewards via the POL bond programme incentivising sustained provision, protocol-level backstop mechanisms that activate in periods of stress, and external LP opportunities that allow non-vault participants to earn yield by contributing to AMM depth. This layered approach ensures that vault token holders always have access to a credible secondary market; the depth may fluctuate with trading conditions, but the structural mechanism is permanently present.

[Note: Detailed \$MINE assurance layer parameters, including backstop trigger conditions and POL treasury allocation, are in preparation to be published as a companion document prior to platform launch.]{.mark}

← Fee StructureThree-Entity Structure →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
REGULATORY / Three-Entity Structure

7.1 Three-Entity Corporate Structure

The Minestarters protocol operates through a Bermuda-centric three-entity architecture designed for regulatory clarity, tax efficiency, and investor protection:

— Figure 5: Three-Entity Corporate Structure —

Minestarters Incubator (Dubai DMCC): The project origination and due diligence hub. Receives mining company applications, conducts the 8-stage funnel, selects projects for vault inclusion, and charges a 5% incubator fee on vault size.

Minestarters Platform (Bermuda, DABA Licensed): The on-chain platform operator. Deploys smart contracts, issues vault tokens (ERC-20), manages investor onboarding/KYC, routes capital (majority to custody, defined portion to AMM), and collects platform fees.

Bermuda Top Co + SAC: The custodian layer. Top Co serves as fiat gateway (USDC↔USD off/on-ramp, 0.5% annual + 0.3% on/off-ramp fee). The Bermuda Segregated Accounts Company (SAC) holds mining equity investments (and other mining financial instruments) in dedicated segregated accounts with statutory ring-fencing; each vault maps to one SAC account.

← $MINE Liquidity AssuranceBermuda DABA Licensing →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
REGULATORY / Bermuda DABA Licensing

7.2 Bermuda DABA Licensing

The Minestarters Platform operates under the Bermuda Digital Asset Business Act (DABA), one of the world's most comprehensive regulatory frameworks for digital asset businesses. Bermuda's regulatory clarity, combined with its established financial services infrastructure and zero corporate income tax, makes it an ideal jurisdiction for a DABA-licensed tokenized investment platform. The DABA framework provides clear guidelines for token issuance, custody, AML/KYC, and consumer protection.

← Three-Entity StructureSAC Ring-Fencing →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
REGULATORY / SAC Ring-Fencing

7.3 SAC Ring-Fencing

The Bermuda Segregated Accounts Company (SAC) structure provides statutory ring-fencing of vault assets. Each vault maps to a dedicated segregated account within the SAC, ensuring that the assets and liabilities of one vault are legally separated from those of another. This structure is analogous to the "ring-fenced assets in Swiss SPVs" model used by traditional fund structures, but with the additional benefit of Bermuda's statutory protections for segregated accounts.

← Bermuda DABA LicensingAML/KYC Framework →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
REGULATORY / AML/KYC Framework

7.4 AML/KYC Framework

The platform implements comprehensive AML/KYC through integration with Sumsub for identity verification and sanctions screening. All investors must complete KYC verification, provide source of funds documentation for large investments, and maintain ongoing compliance. The platform conducts periodic re-verification for high-risk profiles and maintains a complete audit trail for regulatory reporting.

← SAC Ring-FencingJurisdictional Considerations →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
REGULATORY / Jurisdictional Considerations

7.5 Jurisdictional Considerations

Vault tokens may be classified as securities in certain jurisdictions. The Minestarters platform restricts access based on jurisdictional eligibility, with initial focus on jurisdictions where DABA licensing provides regulatory clarity. Restricted jurisdictions (including the United States pending further regulatory guidance) are blocked at the onboarding stage. The platform maintains a dynamic jurisdictional compliance matrix that is updated as regulatory frameworks evolve.

7.6 Governance & Risk Management

Governance parameters including fee rates, vault parameters, curator approval, and platform policy are managed by the founding team during the initial protocol phase. This ensures execution speed and geological judgement during the critical early period when the platform is establishing its first vaults and proving the model. The governance framework is designed to evolve toward broader stakeholder participation as the protocol matures and the \$MINE token ecosystem develops, with governance voting rights available to \$MINE holders on defined platform-level decisions.

← AML/KYC FrameworkBlockchain Infrastructure →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
TECHNOLOGY / Blockchain Infrastructure

8.1 Blockchain Infrastructure

Smart contracts are deployed on an EVM-compatible chain, with final Layer-2 selection in progress across Ethereum L2s, Solana, and Coinbase\'s Base platform. Selection criteria include transaction cost, settlement finality, smart contract tooling maturity, and institutional ecosystem support. The platform uses upgradeable proxy patterns with timelocks and dynamic gas strategies for cost-efficient operation. Critical operations target confirmation depths that ensure settlement finality appropriate for the value of transactions being processed.

[Note: Final blockchain selection and deployment architecture will be confirmed in the technical specification.]{.mark}

← Jurisdictional ConsiderationsData Architecture →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
TECHNOLOGY / Data Architecture

8.2 Data Architecture

The NAV tracking engine ingests and analyses 48+ document types from mining companies across NI 43-101 (Canada/SEDAR), S-K 1300 (USA/SEC EDGAR), and JORC/PERC (Australia/Europe) reporting standards. Project documentation is verified through trusted oracle services before feeding into on-chain NAV calculations. The data architecture is designed for continuous expansion as new reporting jurisdictions and document types are added. Automated ESG tracking is integrated into the reporting framework from launch.

← Blockchain InfrastructureSecurity Architecture →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
TECHNOLOGY / Security Architecture

8.3 Security Architecture

All smart contracts undergo independent third-party audit prior to mainnet deployment. The security framework includes multi-signature requirements (3-of-5 for deployment, 4-of-5 with 48-hour timelock for upgrades), a bug bounty programme for ongoing vulnerability discovery, rate limiting and circuit breakers for emergency situations, on-chain transaction logs with off-chain tamper-evident event logging, and document hash storage on-chain for verification integrity. The platform operates on a permissioned basis only KYC-verified addresses can interact with vault contracts and the AMM provides an additional security layer against anonymous attack vectors.

← Data ArchitectureMarket Position →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
COMPETITIVE / Market Position

9.1 Market Position

The competitive landscape for tokenised mining investment divides along two axes: asset specificity and portfolio structure. Every mining-specific tokenisation to date has been a single-asset or single-jurisdiction offering one company, one commodity, one set of risks. Every diversified, institutional-grade RWA protocol operates outside mining entirely in treasuries, private credit, or structured products. The intersection of mining-domain expertise and diversified, liquid portfolio infrastructure is unoccupied. Minestarters sit in that intersection.

This is not a positioning claim built on a whitepaper diagram. It is observable in the market. The pipeline already includes 15 active projects across five commodities and seven countries, sourced through three established curator networks, with a combined pipeline capital value exceeding \$3 billion. The custody architecture is purpose-built around Bermuda\'s SAC framework. The compliance stack is designed for DABA licensing. The liquidity mechanism ARTs and a protocol-managed AMM is engineered specifically for the illiquidity profile of exploration equity. None of this infrastructure exists elsewhere, because no one else has built it.

9.2 Defensibility

First-mover advantage in tokenised mining exploration is meaningful for three reasons, each of which compounds over time.

Domain expertise cannot be a shortcut. Vault curation requires specialist geological knowledge, the ability to evaluate drill data, resource models, metallurgical risk, jurisdictional permitting complexity, and management quality at the pre-revenue stage. This expertise is embedded in the Minestarters team and curator programme, built through decades of hands-on exploration and development across multiple continents. A generalist RWA protocol cannot acquire this capability through a partnership announcement or a hire. The expertise moat is the deepest because it is the hardest to replicate and the slowest to erode.

Liquidity creates a network effect. Each new vault seeds the AMM with liquidity from its fundraise allocation. As more vaults launch, aggregate liquidity depth increases, attracting more investors because their positions are more tradeable, and attracting more mining companies because the platform offers a credibly liquid capital source. The first protocol to establish meaningful liquidity depth in tokenised exploration equity will be structurally difficult to displace.

Data and track record accumulate. Every vault generates proprietary data geological assessments, NAV histories, project outcome data, curator performance metrics, and secondary market trading patterns. Over time, this dataset becomes a valuation and risk-pricing asset that no new entrant can replicate. The data moat reinforces the expertise moat: better data enables better curation, which produces better outcomes, which attracts more deal flow and more capital.

← Security Architecture8-Stage Project Funnel →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
COMPETITIVE / 8-Stage Project Funnel

9.3 The 8-Stage Project Funnel

Every mining project undergoes a rigorous 8-stage funnel managed by the Minestarters Incubator. The funnel has a \<1% conversion rate from initial sourcing to capital deployment, ensuring only the highest-quality opportunities reach investor capital.

— Figure 7: 8-Stage Project Funnel —

Stage Name Owner Key Activities

1 Sourcing Curators Network outreach, direct submissions, market research

2 Initial Junior Jurisdiction, commodity, stage, team Screening Mining credentials review Analyst

3 Preliminary DD Junior Documentation review, NDA execution, Mining initial risk assessment Analyst

4 Technical DD Senior Geological evaluation, resource Mining estimates, exploration plans Analyst

5 Commercial DD Mining Valuation modeling, terms Director negotiation, legal structure

6 Investment Committee Final approval, terms finalization, Committee risk sign-off

7 Vault Allocation Team Risk/return matching, portfolio balancing across vault

8 Capital SAC Equity acquisition via SAC Deployment segregated account, NAV initialization

← Market PositionCurator Program →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
COMPETITIVE / Curator Program

9.4 Curator Program

The quality of a vault is determined before a single token is minted it is determined by who selects the projects that go into it. Minestarters does not rely on inbound deal flow or generic screening platforms to build vault portfolios. It operates a dedicated curator network: a group of mining industry professionals, geologists, and investment specialists who source, evaluate, and recommend projects for vault inclusion.

Who the curators are. Curators are practising geologists, mining engineers, and sector-focused investment professionals with direct operational experience in exploration and development. They are people who have walked drill sites, read core logs, assessed jurisdictional risk firsthand, and funded or built mines. The founding curator network operates through three established channels: the Nally network, Power Metal Resources, and TerraZar Holdings collectively spanning gold, copper, lithium, base metals, and industrial minerals across multiple continents.

How curation works. Curators submit projects they have identified and assessed to the Minestarters incubator for formal evaluation through the due diligence funnel. A curator does not simply forward a pitch deck they provide a geological thesis, a view on the project\'s position on the development curve, an assessment of management capability, and a recommendation on appropriate capital allocation. The incubator team then applies its own independent evaluation before any project is approved for vault inclusion. This two-layer process curator recommendation followed by incubator verification is designed to filter aggressively. The target conversion rate from initial sourcing to vault placement is well below one in ten.

Incentive alignment. Curators earn success-based compensation when projects they have sourced are placed into vaults and subsequently generate returns. They do not earn fees for submitting projects, for volume of deal flow, or for activity. The incentive structure rewards quality and outcome, not quantity ensuring that curators are motivated to submit only the projects they believe have genuine merit, not to flood the pipeline with marginal opportunities.

Why this scales. As more curators join the network and more vaults launch, the breadth and quality of deal flow compounds. Each new curator brings their own professional network, jurisdictional knowledge, and project pipeline. The network becomes increasingly difficult to replicate over time a geologist with thirty years of West African exploration experience and a network of local partners represents a sourcing advantage that cannot be hired overnight or built from a database. The curator network is designed to be Minestarters\' deepest competitive moat: the reason the best projects come to this platform rather than seeking traditional financing.

← 8-Stage Project FunnelRoadmap →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
Roadmap

10. Roadmap

10.1 Phase 1: Foundation (2025--Q2 2026)

The foundation phase establishes the corporate, legal, and technical infrastructure required to bring the platform to market. Minestarters Dubai (DMCC) is operational as the incubator entity handling project sourcing, due diligence, and curator program management. The Bermuda DABA licence application for Minestarters Tech and SAC incorporation for RWA Custody Top Co are in progress. Blockchain selection is being finalized across Layer-2 options including Ethereum, Solana, and Coinbase\'s Base platform.

Core smart contract development is underway vault contracts, token issuance logic, milestone-gated funding mechanisms, and AMM architecture with independent security audit scheduled prior to any mainnet deployment. The curator program has been seeded through three established networks (Nally, Power Metal, TerraZar), and the project pipeline already includes 15 active projects with a combined capital value exceeding \$3 billion across five commodities and seven countries. Platform UI/UX design and wireframe finalisation are in progress alongside KYC/AML integration with Sumsub for compliant investor onboarding. This whitepaper and the accompanying institutional materials mark the beginning of formal investor outreach.

10.2 Phase 2: Platform Launch (Q3--Q4 2026)

The launch phase brings the first products to market, beginning with mineral token aggregation tokenised exposure to physical mineral assets providing immediate liquidity and a functioning on-chain product while the longer-duration exploration vault infrastructure completes its regulatory and technical build-out.

Smart contracts deploy to mainnet following completion of audit. The NAV tracking module launches in its initial version, prioritising NI 43-101 and S-K 1300 document types as the most common reporting standards in the pipeline\'s target geographies. Automated ESG tracking is integrated into the reporting framework from launch.

Vault 1 opens for qualified investor subscription, with the target raise determined by the capital requirements of the selected project portfolio. Capital deployment to portfolio companies operates through milestone-gated smart contracts funding is released in tranches as projects achieve predetermined technical and commercial milestones, with subsequent stages withheld if a company fails to deliver on agreed targets. This automates much of the capital oversight that traditionally requires manual intervention and protracted negotiation.

The permissioned AMM activates with Asset-Referenced Token liquidity, enabling secondary market trading for vault token holders from the point of deployment. Protocol-owned liquidity and vault-owned liquidity mechanisms provide baseline market depth.

10.3 Phase 3: Scale (2027+)

The scale phase expands the platform across every dimension: more vaults, more products, more geographies, and deeper liquidity.

Multiple vaults operate concurrently, targeting three to five active vaults with diversified portfolio theses across commodity types and jurisdictions. Commodity coverage extends beyond the initial pipeline to include lithium, cobalt, rare earths, and other energy-transition minerals. Geographic coverage broadens as new curator relationships bring deal flow from additional jurisdictions.

The \$MINE protocol token launches with governance functionality, enabling stakeholder participation in platform-level decisions. Token holders can stake \$MINE for defined periods 3, 6, 9, or 12 months earning yield paid in \$MINE tokens. Supply management mechanisms activate, including buyback programs, token burns, and utility sinks designed to align circulating supply with platform growth.

The product scope expands progressively along the mining development lifecycle: from exploration-stage equity into tokenised royalties, streaming agreements, convertible equity structures, development-stage funding, and operational mine stakes. The goal is a comprehensive marketplace that finances projects from first drill to steady-state production.

The platform explores DeFi composability, integrations vault tokens as collateral in lending protocols, yield strategies for idle vault capital and institutional distribution partnerships to broaden the capital base beyond direct platform subscribers. The platform targets capturing at least 1% of the mining sector\'s annual capital flow equivalent to more than \$2 billion annually by connecting global qualified investors with curated exploration and development opportunities.

← Curator ProgramTeam & Governance →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
Team & Governance

11. Team & Governance

Minestarters exists because its founding team has built mining companies, raised Web3 capital, and engineered vault architectures separately and recognised that these three capabilities had never been brought together in a single platform. The team is not a group of technologists who discovered mining, or miners who discovered blockchain. It is a deliberate combination of deep domain expertise across the three disciplines required to execute this protocol: geological curation, decentralised finance, and smart contract engineering.

Marcel Nally - Chairman

Founder of Minestarters. Marcel is a geologist with over 25 years of hands-on exploration and development experience across eleven countries, including Russia, Indonesia, Zambia, Spain, Finland, Chile, Peru, Ethiopia, Republic of Congo, Ireland, and the United Kingdom. He co-founded Moxico Resources plc, which operates a copper mine in Zambia one of eleven projects he was involved with prior to their development that went on to become producing mines or advanced projects, including Veduga (3M oz Au) and Asacha (1M oz Au) for Trans-Siberian Gold, and Mimbula (800,000t Cu) for Moxico. Marcel graduated with a BSc in Mining Geology from the Royal School of Mines, Imperial College London. He has been involved in two previous IPOs and recognised in 2016 that blockchain could fundamentally reshape how exploration capital is raised, founding the Minestarters concept that year. Marcel leads project selection and curates the vault portfolios. His interest in blockchain dates to 2013.

Edward Ryall - Chief Executive Officer

DeFi pioneer and experienced capital raiser. Edward has raised \$10M+ in Web3 capital from Coinbase Ventures, Fenbushi Capital, and Animoca Brands. He is co-founder of Neptune Mutual, an on-chain parametric cover protocol. Edward brings deep experience in building ventures within highly regulated technology environments and navigating the intersection of decentralised finance and institutional compliance. Adjunct Professor, Executive MBA. He leads the commercial strategy, investor relations, and regulatory positioning of the Minestarters platform.

Reuben Rodrigues - Chief Technology Officer

Smart contract architect and vault infrastructure specialist. Reuben has designed vault and liquidity pool architectures securing \$30M+ in total value locked. He brings founder-level engineering experience in smart contract security, DeFi protocol design, and on-chain financial infrastructure. Reuben leads the technical development of Minestarters\' smart contract vaults, AMM, NAV tracking engine, and platform architecture.

Three-Entity Governance Structure

The platform operates through three purpose-built entities, each with distinct responsibilities and jurisdictional logic:

  • Minestarters Dubai (DMCC) serves as the incubator and project

    investor, the off-chain operational hub for the project funnel, due diligence, curator program management, and incubation services to mining companies. This is where geological and commercial judgement is applied before capital is committed.

  • Minestarters Tech (Bermuda, DABA-licensed) is the regulated platform

    operator and on-chain token issuer. It deploys smart contract vaults and the AMM, manages investor onboarding, and operates the NAV tracking and reporting engine.

  • RWA Custody Top Co (Bermuda SAC) is the custodian of a Segregated

    Accounts Company that holds the underlying mining equity positions in ring-fenced SPV cells per vault. It provides statutory asset segregation, fiat gateway and on/off ramp functionality, and segregated accounts per project.

Governance model. Governance parameters including fee rates, vault parameters, curator approval, and platform policy are managed by the founding team during the initial protocol phase. The governance framework is designed to evolve toward broader stakeholder participation as the protocol matures and the \$MINE token ecosystem develops, but the founding team retains direct control during the critical early period when execution speed and geological judgement are more important than decentralised consensus.

← RoadmapRisk Factors →
Minestarters · Whitepaper v1.0 · March 2026 · For informational purposes only
Risk Factors

12. Risk Factors

Investment in Minestarters vault tokens involves substantial risk, including the potential loss of all invested capital. Prospective participants should carefully consider the following:

  • Exploration Risk. Mining exploration is inherently speculative.

    The majority of exploration projects do not result in economically viable mineral deposits. Valuable discoveries are rare --- and it is common for market pricing to reflect speculative potential rather than geological probability. Vault diversification across multiple projects mitigates but does not eliminate this risk.

  • Commodity Price Risk. All mineral commodity projects are

    leveraged to commodity prices --- margins expand faster than underlying prices in rising markets, and contract equally fast in declining markets. Prolonged commodity downturns can reduce vault NAV regardless of geological success. Mining EBITDA margins have historically demonstrated significant cyclicality.

  • Funding & Dilution Risk. Individual portfolio companies within a

    vault may undertake further capital raises that dilute the vault\'s equity position. The timing, terms, and pricing of such fundraising are outside the vault\'s control. The vault structure addresses this at the portfolio level by deploying a fixed capital pool at creation, but project-level dilution remains a risk.

  • Political & Jurisdictional Risk. Mineral deposits cannot be

    moved. Mining companies are reliant on host governments for permits to operate and may face changes to tax regimes, royalty structures, export restrictions, or regulatory requirements that erode project economics. Resource nationalism is a persistent and often underestimated risk across mining jurisdictions.

  • Valuation Risk. Exploration-stage assets are inherently
